summary:
The Manchu Invasions of Korea (1627 and 1636–1637 AD) were the military campaigns through which the rising Manchu state (soon to become the [[event:2337|Qing dynasty]]) forced Korea to renounce its tributary relationship with the Ming dynasty and acknowledge Manchu supremacy — a politically traumatic transformation for the Joseon kingdom, which had deep cultural and political ties to China's Ming dynasty. The first invasion (1627) forced the Joseon court to acknowledge the Manchus as brothers and enter a trade relationship; when Joseon refused to honor this relationship fully and continued supporting Ming, the second invasion (1636) saw the Manchu force (now formally the Qing dynasty under Hong Taiji) advance rapidly to besiege Namhansanseong fortress.

King Injo of Joseon was forced to surrender in January 1637, performing the 'three kneelings and nine prostrations' (samjeon gugo) before the Qing emperor — a ritual of complete submission that Korean historians have described as the most humiliating moment in Joseon history. The treaty required Korea to accept Qing suzerainty, sever all ties with the Ming dynasty, provide military contingents for Qing campaigns (including helping in the final conquest of Ming China), and send royal princes as hostages to the Qing court. Despite formal Qing suzerainty, Joseon retained considerable internal autonomy; the dynasty continued to use Ming calendar dates internally for decades as a form of cultural-political protest, and a deep private loyalty to Ming civilization persisted among the Yangban elite — the ideology of Joseonjung-hua, positioning Korea as the true inheritor of Chinese civilization after the Manchu 'barbarians' had conquered China.
